title: Dog Blood in the White of the Eye: Causes and Triage Advice
meta_desc: Seeing blood in your dog's eye? Learn about subconjunctival hemorrhage, why it happens, and when you should contact your veterinarian for an exam.
content_html:
What is Blood in the White of the Eye?

Finding a bright red patch on the white part of your dog's eye (the sclera) can be alarming. This condition is typically known as a subconjunctival hemorrhage. It occurs when a tiny blood vessel beneath the clear surface of the eye (the conjunctiva) breaks and leaks. While it looks dramatic and frightening, it is essentially a bruise on the surface of the eye.

Is It an Emergency?

The urgency level for blood in the white of the eye is generally considered Medium. While the red spot itself is often harmless and painless, it can sometimes be a symptom of a more serious underlying health issue such as trauma, high blood pressure, or a blood clotting disorder. You should schedule a veterinary appointment within 24 hours to ensure there is no internal damage or systemic illness causing the bleed.

Why a Photo Helps with Triage

Taking a clear, well-lit photo of your dog's eye can be incredibly helpful for your veterinary triage team. A photo allows the professional to distinguish between a surface bruise and blood inside the eye (hyphema), which is a much more serious emergency. It also provides a baseline to see if the redness is spreading or receding during follow-up checks.

faqs.question: Does blood in the eye mean my dog is in pain?
faqs.answer: A simple subconjunctival hemorrhage is usually not painful. However, if your dog is squinting, pawing at the eye, or has excessive tearing, it could indicate a more serious injury like a corneal ulcer or uveitis.

faqs.question: What are common causes of this condition?
faqs.answer: Common causes include blunt trauma, rough play, intense coughing, or even straining on a leash. In some cases, it can be a sign of systemic issues like hypertension or vasculitis.

faqs.question: How long does it take for the blood to disappear?
faqs.answer: Similar to a bruise on the skin, the blood is gradually reabsorbed by the body. This process typically takes between 7 to 14 days, and the spot may change color from bright red to a duller shade before fading completely.
